title: Non-uniform motion
paperName: Paper 1: Multiple Choice / Paper 2: AS Level Structured Questions
summary: Master non-uniform motion for Cambridge 9702: variable acceleration, drag forces, terminal velocity and graph interpretation explained clearly.
learningObjectives: Explain how and why acceleration varies when a resultant force changes with velocity, including the effect of drag forces on a falling object
Sketch and interpret displacement–time, velocity–time and acceleration–time graphs for non-uniform motion, identifying key features such as terminal velocity
Apply Newton's second law to objects experiencing non-constant resultant forces, showing how net force and acceleration decrease as drag increases

simpleExplanation.title: What is non-uniform motion?
simpleExplanation.summary: Non-uniform motion simply means the acceleration of an object is not constant — it changes over time. This happens whenever the resultant force acting on the object is changing, for example because air resistance (drag) increases as an object speeds up. The most common exam example is an object falling through a fluid and eventually reaching terminal velocity.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think about opening a car door on a motorway — the harder you push, the faster it opens, but the air resistance pushing back gets stronger the wider it opens, making it harder and harder to push. Eventually the door moves at a steady speed where your push exactly balances the air resistance. That steady speed is terminal velocity.
steps: Recall that non-uniform motion means changing acceleration, caused by a changing resultant force
Learn the sequence for a falling object: large acceleration at first → drag increases → acceleration decreases → terminal velocity when drag = weight
Practise sketching the v–t graph for terminal velocity: a curve with decreasing gradient that flattens to a horizontal line
Check you can link each graph type (s–t, v–t, a–t) to the physical situation — examiners love asking you to describe what a specific graph shape tells you

sections.content: In Topic 3.1 you studied uniform acceleration — nice straight lines on v–t graphs. Real life is messier. Whenever the **resultant force** on an object changes as it moves, the **acceleration is non-uniform**: it varies with time. The classic Cambridge example is an object falling through air or another fluid, where **drag** grows as speed increases. Understanding this topic means you can explain terminal velocity, sketch the correct graph shapes, and apply Newton's second law confidently — skills tested on both Paper 1 MCQs and Paper 2 extended questions.
Why does acceleration change? — The role of drag

For an object moving through a fluid (liquid or gas), the **drag force** (sometimes called air resistance or viscous drag) opposes the motion and increases with speed. At low speeds drag is small; at high speeds it can be very large. This means the **resultant force** — and therefore the acceleration — is not constant.

For a falling object of mass m:
• Resultant downward force = Weight − Drag = mg − F_drag
• By Newton's second law: a = (mg − F_drag) / m

As the object accelerates, F_drag increases, so the resultant force decreases, and the acceleration decreases. This continues until F_drag = mg, at which point the resultant force is zero and the object moves at a constant velocity — this is called **terminal velocity**.

Resultant force: F_net = mg − F_drag    →    Acceleration: a = (mg − F_drag) / m

At terminal velocity: F_drag = mg    →    a = 0    →    v = v_terminal (constant)
Graphs for non-uniform motion — what they look like and why

Cambridge examiners frequently ask you to sketch or describe graphs. Here is what each graph looks like for an object falling and reaching terminal velocity:

**Velocity–time (v–t) graph:**
The curve starts at v = 0 with a steep (nearly straight) gradient equal to g. The gradient (= acceleration) progressively decreases as drag increases. The curve bends and eventually becomes horizontal at v = v_terminal. The gradient is always positive but decreasing — it is never negative in this scenario.

**Acceleration–time (a–t) graph:**
Starts at a = g (≈ 9.81 m s⁻²) at t = 0. Decreases in a curve (not a straight line) towards zero. Never goes negative. Reaches zero asymptotically at terminal velocity.

**Displacement–time (s–t) graph:**
Curves upward with an increasing but decelerating rate of change. The gradient (= velocity) increases but at a decreasing rate. Eventually the gradient becomes constant (straight line) at terminal velocity.

**Key examiner trap:** The gradient of the v–t graph decreasing does NOT mean the object is slowing down — it is still speeding up, just at a smaller rate. Only when the gradient is zero is the velocity constant.

items: Non-uniform motion occurs when the resultant force — and therefore acceleration — changes over time; this is always caused by a force that depends on velocity (such as drag) or position (such as gravity over large distances)
For a body falling through a fluid: acceleration starts at g, decreases continuously, and reaches zero at terminal velocity; the body never decelerates in free fall through a uniform fluid
Terminal velocity is reached when the drag force exactly equals the weight of the object; at this point resultant force = 0 and velocity is constant

On a v–t graph, non-uniform acceleration shows as a curve; the gradient at any point gives the instantaneous acceleration — a decreasing positive gradient means speeding up but with reducing acceleration
A heavier object of the same shape reaches a higher terminal velocity because it requires a greater drag force to balance its weight; drag increases with speed until the balance is achieved
When sketching graphs in Paper 2, always label axes with quantity and unit, mark any key values (e.g. v_terminal, g), and ensure curves start and end with the correct gradient

sections.question:
A skydiver of total mass 80 kg jumps from a stationary helicopter. The drag force F_drag on the skydiver varies with velocity v according to F_drag = kv², where k = 0.32 kg m⁻¹.

(a) Show that the terminal velocity is approximately 50 m s⁻¹.
(b) Calculate the resultant force and acceleration when the skydiver's speed is 20 m s⁻¹.
(c) Describe the shape of the velocity–time graph from the moment of jumping until terminal velocity is reached. (g = 9.81 m s⁻²)
sections.solution:
**Part (a) — Terminal velocity:**
At terminal velocity, drag = weight
kv² = mg
0.32 × v² = 80 × 9.81
v² = 784 / 0.32 = 2452.5
v = √2452.5 ≈ 49.5 m s⁻¹ ≈ 50 m s⁻¹ ✓

**Part (b) — At v = 20 m s⁻¹:**
Drag force = kv² = 0.32 × (20)² = 0.32 × 400 = 128 N
Weight = mg = 80 × 9.81 = 784.8 N ≈ 785 N
Resultant force = 785 − 128 = 657 N (downward)
Acceleration = F / m = 657 / 80 = 8.2 m s⁻² (downward)

Note: this is less than g (9.81 m s⁻²) because drag is already significant at 20 m s⁻¹.

**Part (c) — Description of v–t graph:**
• At t = 0, v = 0; the gradient equals g (≈ 9.81 m s⁻²) because drag is zero initially
• As speed increases, drag increases, reducing the resultant force and therefore the acceleration
• The gradient of the graph decreases continuously — the curve bends away from a straight line
• The curve approaches the terminal velocity (≈ 50 m s⁻¹) asymptotically — the gradient approaches zero
• The graph is always concave downward (gradient always positive but decreasing)

sections.content: In Paper 2, when asked to 'describe the motion' from a graph, always comment on THREE things: the value or direction of the velocity, whether it is increasing or decreasing, and what is happening to the acceleration (i.e. comment on the gradient and how it changes). Writing 'the object accelerates' alone will not score full marks — you must say something like 'the object accelerates at a decreasing rate until it reaches a constant terminal velocity'. Also watch out for MCQs that show an a–t graph starting below g — this would mean there is an initial drag force, which is physically impossible if the object starts from rest. The acceleration must start at g when v = 0.

faq.q: What is non-uniform motion in Cambridge 9702 Physics and how is it examined?
faq.a: Non-uniform motion in Cambridge 9702 refers to situations where an object's acceleration changes over time because the resultant force acting on it is not constant. The most common exam context is a body falling through a fluid where drag increases with speed, eventually leading to terminal velocity. It is examined in Paper 1 as multiple-choice questions on graph interpretation and in Paper 2 as structured questions requiring sketch graphs, calculations of resultant force, and written descriptions of motion. Our free course includes worked examples and past-paper marking practice specifically for this topic.

faq.q: How do you explain terminal velocity for Cambridge A Level Physics?
faq.a: Terminal velocity occurs when the drag force on a moving object exactly equals the driving force (for a falling object, this is its weight). At this point, the resultant force is zero, so by Newton's second law the acceleration is also zero, and the object moves at a constant, maximum velocity. The key point examiners look for is that terminal velocity is not reached instantly — the object accelerates (at a decreasing rate) until the forces balance. Our premium lesson on non-uniform motion walks through this step by step with graph sketches and a full worked example.

faq.q: Why does the acceleration decrease as an object falls through air in 9702 Physics?
faq.a: As the object falls and speeds up, the drag force (which depends on velocity) increases. This means the resultant downward force — weight minus drag — gets smaller over time. Since acceleration = resultant force / mass (Newton's second law), a smaller resultant force means a smaller acceleration. The acceleration does not become negative; it simply decreases from its initial value of g towards zero. This is a very common source of confusion in Paper 2 answers, and our free revision lesson covers it with clear graph descriptions and examiner tips.
